coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of graphene-quantum-dots decorated metal-free carbon nitride for effective electrocatalysts in redox-mediated electrolyte
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ying
Yan, Bo
Zhuo, Haiyue
Ma, Churong
Chen, Kai
coredata.dc:description: Defect engineering of N/C3N4 two-dimensional material provides an efficient way to tune the electronic structures and make more active sites for electrocatalysis, which is attractive for electrochemical energy storage applications. Other than modification of heteroatoms on the top of C3N4 two-dimensional material as a photosensitizer, electrolyte design is very desirable to enrich the research area and provides a wide range of choices for diverse applications. Herein, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated N doped g-C3N4 electrocatalysts for water splitting were fabricated, which exhibited enhanced electron transfer efficiency, better electrocatalytic performance, and long-term stability. The electrochemical performances of GQDs@C3N4+x in neutral, acidic, alkaline, and redox-mediated electrolytes were investigated, respectively. The GQDs@C3N4+x electrode displays excellent activity toward electrocatalytic reaction in redox-mediated solution. The increase in electrocatalytic performance could be attributed to the reaction of redox species with the surface holes and the defects of GQDs@C3N4+x , leading to a better separation of the excited electron-hole pairs. Thus, this work greatly broadens the application prospects of carbon nitrogen complex by rational defect engineering and electrolyte regulation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalysis
Graphene quantum dots
Defect engineering
Electrolyte regulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon nitride series catalysts

Basic and applied basic research project of Guangzhou
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62104081) and Basic and Applied Basic Research Project of Guangzhou (No. 202201010142).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) C3N4, (b) C3N4+x and (c) GQDs@C3N4+x . TEM images of (a1) C3N4, (b1) C3N4+x and (c1) GQDs@C3N4+x . Insets of (a1) and (b1) are the selected area electron diffraction patterns. HRTEM images of (a2) C3N4, (b2) C3N4+x , and (c2) GQDs@C3N4+x . Inset of (c2) is the larger magnification of GQDs@C3N4+x . (d) The illustration of crystal structures of carbon nitride materials.

XRD patterns of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x . Inset of (a) shows the magnification of (002) peak profiles. (b) Raman spectra of C3N4, C3N4+x
. Insert of (b) shows the partial magnification. (c) UV–vis spectra of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x . Insert of (c) is hν vs. (αhν)2 plots. AFM images of (e) C3N4+x and (f) GQDs@C3N4+x and the related height profiles along the dash lines in the corresponding top panels.

High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) N 1 s and (b) C 1 s of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x samples. (c) is the partial magnification of (b). (d) The valence-band edge (VBE) spectra for g-C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x. Inset is the local magnification of (d). (e) PL spectra of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x . Excitation wavelength (λex) is 385 nm. (f) TRF spectra of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x .

(a) LSV curves of C3N4+x , C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x with scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in 0.10 M NaOH. Inset is the Tafel plots of C3N4+x , C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x . (b) AC impedance curves and (c) Mott-Schottky plots of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x in 0.10 M NaOH. (d) Stability tests of C3N4, C3N4+x , GQDs@C3N4+x and C3N4 in 0.1 M NaOH.

(a) Electrochemical properties of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x at 1.8 V vs. RHE potential in different electrolytes. (b) Summary of onset potentials with GQDs@C3N4+x electrode in different electrolytes. (c) AC impedance curves and (d) Mott-Schottky plots of C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x in 0.10 M Na2S/Na2S2O3 electrolytes.
The band alignment and carrier transport mechanism of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x .
The fitting lifetime (τ) of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x .
The fitting results of equivalent circuit impedance analysis.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Rs: resistance value of the external circuit; CPE: constant phase angle element; R1: charge transfer impedance; C: capacitive reactance; W: Warburg impedance.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of graphene-quantum-dots decorated metal-free carbon nitride for effective electrocatalysts in redox-mediated electrolyte

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Fiber Sensing and Communications, Institute of Photonics Technology, Jinan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Defect engineering of N/C3N4 two-dimensional material provides an efficient way to tune the electronic structures and make more active sites for electrocatalysis, which is attractive for electrochemical energy storage applications. Other than modification of heteroatoms on the top of C3N4 two-dimensional material as a photosensitizer, electrolyte design is very desirable to enrich the research area and provides a wide range of choices for diverse applications. Herein, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated N doped g-C3N4 electrocatalysts for water splitting were fabricated, which exhibited enhanced electron transfer efficiency, better electrocatalytic performance, and long-term stability. The electrochemical performances of GQDs@C3N4+x in neutral, acidic, alkaline, and redox-mediated electrolytes were investigated, respectively. The GQDs@C3N4+x electrode displays excellent activity toward electrocatalytic reaction in redox-mediated solution. The increase in electrocatalytic performance could be attributed to the reaction of redox species with the surface holes and the defects of GQDs@C3N4+x , leading to a better separation of the excited electron-hole pairs. Thus, this work greatly broadens the application prospects of carbon nitrogen complex by rational defect engineering and electrolyte regulation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical water splitting is widely considered one of the most attractive clean and sustainable technologies, due to its simplicity and high energy conversion efficiency. Water splitting is a thermodynamically unfavored reaction with the overall efficiency of the process hindered by the large overpotentials for the hydrogen evolution and oxygen evolution reaction on most catalytic materials [1,2]. Therefore, electrode materials, which play an important role in electrochemical catalytic performance, have attracted much attention to overcome the bottleneck and for the complete utilization of solar light [3,4]. In parallel with the search for a potential substitute for noble metal and exploration of the unique characteristics of new metal-free catalysts, there is growing interest in emerging two-dimensional nanomaterials [5].

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) two-dimensional nanomaterial has unique physicochemical properties, promising catalytic activities, high in-plane nitrogen content, and environmentally friendly features, which has been explored in the field of photocatalysis, bioimaging, CO2 reduction, and other energy conversion processes [6–8]. Despite this, g-C3N4 is mainly restricted to electrochemical applications due to its inherent low electronic conductivity and insufficient catalytic centers [9,10]. Researchers are focusing on the defect regulation of g-C3N4 to enhance its electronic properties and performance of electrocatalysis [11]. Nitrogen defect is one of the most common defects in g-C3N4, owing to the high content of N atoms and the layered structure that could provide plentiful and evenly distributed defects [12]. Reasonable defect engineering has effectively shown great potential for enhancing catalytic performance [13,14].

To further improve the photon harvesting range of photocatalysts, researchers focused their discussion on photosensitizers. Photosensitizer can increase the density of photogenerated electrons on the conduction band of the photocatalyst with large band gaps thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity [15]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which exhibit unique up-conversion photoluminescence, high-efficiency broad-spectrum absorption, and excellent photo-induced electron transfer ability have been proven to be an effective photosensitizer [16]. An increasing body of research has attempted to couple semiconductors with GQDs to fabricate photocatalysts to improve their photocatalytic performance. Mondal et al. synthesized GQDs decorated TiO2 nanorod arrays and established the role of GQDs as a photosensitizer for enhanced photoelectrochemical activity [17]. Ray et al. synthesized the N-GQD quantum dot-sensitized solar cells to obtain a much superior performance of 5.3% [18].

Besides the catalyst, the electrochemical performance is also affected by the electrolyte properties, including the ion type and ion concentration. The electrolyte properties are related to the electrolyte’s ion conductivity that have a strong effect on capacitance. In general, the electrolyte can affect cycle life, catalytic performance and operating conditions of electrolytic cells [19]. Suitable electrolytes could form a good solid-electrolyte interphase layer, facilitating ion transfer through the interface and improving the cycling and catalytic rate of the electrolytic cell [20,21]. Furthermore, the interaction between the electrolyte and the electrode material can significantly influence the solid-electrolyte interphase state. Hence, a better understanding of the electrolyte’s role in electrocatalysis and the rational design of electrolytes are of great importance for developing highly efficient electrocatalytic systems. Recently, the solvation structure and local dynamics of the electrolyte, as a function of the liquid components and concentrations thereof, have garnered increased attention. Some reports are devoted to the electrochemical performance in different types of electrolytes, such as Co x P, FexNi1-xP and Co x Ni y N, where electrode materials exhibited different electrocatalytic properties in electrolytes with different pH values [22–24]. Besides, the usage of redox-mediated electrolytes could effectively increase the charge capacity of the electric double-layer capacitors, which is beneficial for the enhancement of electrochemical properties [25]. Up to now, there is still a lack of systematic studies focused on comprehensively investigating the role of different electrolytes in electrocatalysis.

Herein, we designed a metal-free graphene quantum dots decorated ultra-thin porous g-C3N4+x heterostructure. The GQDs@C3N4+x catalyst exhibited improved electrocatalytic performance compared with both g-C3N4 and N-doped C3N4, attributing to the synergistic effect of structure modification and defect engineering. The influence of different electrolytes on electrocatalysis was also investigated. The g-C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x electrodes exhibited better electrocatalytic performances in redox-mediated electrolyte compared with those in alkaline, neutral, and acidic electrolytes. The redox-mediated electrolyte could provide protons to participate in the oxidation-reduction reaction. These defect engineering and electrolyte regulation strategies represent viable approaches for the large-scale implementation of metal-free electrocatalysts for efficient energy conversion.

Melamine (C3H6N6, 99.9%, Aladdin), graphite rod (Ai Da Heng Sheng Co. Ltd.), NH4F (99.99%, Macklin), NaF (≥99%, Aladdin), C2H5ONa (20% w/w ethanol solution, Macklin), NaOH (97%, Macklin), Na2S2O3 (99%, Aladdin), Na2S (95%, Aladdin), K4Fe(CN)6 (99.5%, Macklin), C12H25O4SNa (99.0%, Aladdin), KCl (99.8%, Aladdin), 2,3-dihydrofuran (DHF, 99%, Aladdin), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9%, Aladdin).

GQDs were prepared by the electrochemical exfoliation (“Top-down”) method as in our previous report [26]. The electrochemical experiment was performed using a three-electrode system. Graphite rod (φ=10 mm, L = 100 mm) was used as working electrode. Pt was used as the counter and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, respectively. Electrochemical exfoliation of the graphite rod was operated in 0.10 M C2H5ONa solution (100 mL) by cyclic voltammetry in the range of 0–10 V with a scan rate of 0.5 V s−1. The electrochemical process lasted for 2 hours. Then, the supernatant solution of GODs suspension was collected after centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was then dialyzed with 500 D dialysis bag for 48 h. GQDs were dried overnight in a freeze dryer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon nitride series catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: N doped C3N4 was synthesized by a two-step pyrocondensation polymerization approach [27]. First, 3 g C3H6N6 powder in a ceramic crucible was heated to 390 ℃ in a muffle furnace for 1 h with a heating rate of 5 ℃ min−1, further heated to 550 ℃ for 3 h with a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1. Secondly, the as-prepared g-C3N4 powder was grinded with 0.1 g NH4F for 10 min. Then, the mixture was heated to 550 ℃ for another 2 h with a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1 in a vacuum tube furnace. Finally, the reddish-brown colored N doped C3N4 powder was obtained (labeled as C3N4+x ). GQDs decorated C3N4+x was prepared by microwave irradiation. 1 mg mL−1 GQDs solution and 10 mg mL−1 C3N4+x aqueous solution were thoroughly mixed by ultrasonication for 10 min. Then, 20 mL of mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon reactor. The microwave reaction was carried out at 180 ℃ for 30 min. After the product was cooled down naturally, the powder was collected by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and dried in the oven overnight. The obtained GQDs-decorated N doped C3N4 powder was marked as GQDs@C3N4+x . The carbon nitride series materials were loaded in the bare glass carbon electrode for the electrochemical characterization. The 1.0 mg carbon nitride series sample was ultrasound in the mixture solution of 5 μL Nafion and 95 μL deionized water. The 50 µL suspension was dropped on the bare glass carbon electrode with a surface area of 0.28 cm2 (φ=mm).

Scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) images were obtained by Quanta 400FEG (FEI Co. Ltd., Netherlands) with an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. Field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were recorded at TecnaiTM G2 F30 (FEI Co. Ltd., USA) with an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained by using a Bruker multimode V8 system (Bruker Co. Ltd., USA) with the tapping mode after the samples were deposited on a freshly cleaved mica surface by spin coating. X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was carried out on SmartLab (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu-Kα as the radiation source (λ = 0.154 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, K-Alpha+, Thermo fisher Scientific, USA) with a monochromatic Al-Kα source was conducted. The C1s binding energy of adventitious carbon contamination with 284.8 eV was selected as the reference. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was obtained by Frontier FT-IR/NIR/FIR (Frontier, PerkinElmer Inc., USA). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum was recorded at a UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Frontier, PerkinElmer Inc., USA). Raman spectrum was obtained by inVia Reflex (Renishaw, UK) with a He-Ne laser of a wavelength of 514 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL) were recorded on an FLSP920 fluorescence lifetime spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Int., UK). The PL spectrum was excited by a 450 W Xe lamp and the TRPL was tested under the excitation of a nanosecond flashlamp with a wavelength of 406.2 nm (Nf900). The electrochemical test was carried out by an electrochemical workstation (PGSTAT 302 N, Metrohm Autolab, Netherlands) with the FRA model (10 µHz∼1 MHz). All the electrochemical measurements were conducted in a three-electrode cell using a graphite rod as the counter, Ag/AgCl or Hg/HgO as the reference which depends on the pH value of the electrolyte. The samples on the glass carbon electrode were selected as the working electrode.

Fig. 1 illustrates the morphologies of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x samples. C3N4 sample shows a similar layer structure as graphite (Fig. 1a and 1a1), with strong C-N bonds instead of C-C bonds in each layer and weak van der Waals force between layers [28,29]. According to the TEM image (Fig.1a2), g-C3N4 exhibited good crystallinity with the interplanar lattice spacing of 0.34 nm and 0.24 nm corresponding to the (100) and (110) crystal planes, respectively. The thermodynamic instability of 2D nitrogen carbide caused the C3N4 sheets to twist and fold to form 3D fishnet-liked structures, which were shown in Fig. 1b. The stacked structure of C3N4+x endows it with outstanding light-harvesting ability, short charge-to-surface diffusion length and large surface area [30]. In addition, the curly C3N4+x became amorphous after the thermal annealing process (Fig. 1b2). The morphology of GQDs@C3N4+x changed much thinner and curlier after microwave treatment (Fig. 1c and c1). Microwave conditions could provide high energy and high pressure during a short time, which is beneficial for crushing the catalyst as well as creating substantial defects on the surface of the catalyst. The individual GQDs with good crystallinity showed the interplanar lattice spacing of 0.32 nm corresponding to the (002) crystal plane and an average diameter of ca. 8 nm (Fig. S1). After being decorated, GQDs with distorted lattice fringes were well dispersed on the surface of the amorphous C3N4+x (Fig. 1c2), indicating the existence of lattice defects on the surface of the catalyst.

The illustration of Fig. 1d shows the crystal structure of g-C3N4, N doped C3N4 and GQDs@C3N4+x . In g-C3N4, the low coordinated N atoms show oxidation activity in planar while the C atoms show reduction activity between the interlayer. The adjacent thiotriazinone is bonded with bridging carbon and the electrons are easily restricted to the thiotriazinone. The carrier transportation from one heptazine unit to another is limited by the bridging N atoms. Unless the strength of sp2 hybridization near the band edges between the bridging N and adjacent C is significantly large, the intergranular carrier migration would be suppressed [31]. It could be assumed that each heptazine unit behaves independently during the electrocatalytic process unless an alternative configuration strategy can be designed to address these problems. Defects in 2D materials are scattering sources that influence the charge carrier mobility and contact resistance in electronic devices [32]. Hence, N atoms introduced into the tri-s triazine units of g-C3N4 could afford a new channel for carrier migration and modify the carrier mobility as well as facilitate the separation of e−/h+ pairs [33]. Additional GQDs are fixed in the middle of the cage or between adjacent layers. The GQDs could form non-localized π bonds between thiotriazinone structures. The introduction of GQDs is beneficial for the separation of the generated electron-hole pairs and their subsequent transfer to the surface for the desired redox reactions instead of being trapped and recombined [34].

The XRD diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 at 12.6° and 27.6° were ascribed to the (100) and (002) lattice planes with hexagonal phase structure (JCPDS file No. 87–1526), which correspond to the heptazine unit and the stacking structure of layered graphite, respectively ( Fig. 2a). The (002) plane of GQDs@C3N4+x showed an apparent shift to higher angle direction compared with that of C3N4 (Inset of Fig. 3a). The fact that only one diffraction peak shifts instead of all peaks suggests anisotropic contraction of the lattice (e.g. lattice distortion) caused by macroscopic residual stress. FT-IR spectroscopy in Fig. S2 showed that carbon nitride series samples had the same molecular structures. The bands from 1200 to 1700 cm−1 region were associated with C−N heterocycle stretches. The peak at 810 cm−1 was attributed to the typical breathing mode of the tri-s-triazine unit. The band range from 3000 to 3300 cm−1 represented the vibration of N−H [39]. No vibration or stretching peaks of C were detected, which might be due to the low content of GQDs. Hence, Raman spectroscopy is used to further distinguish the differences of carbon nitride series samples in Fig. 2b. The background peak was because of the strong fluorescent of carbon nitride series samples. The characteristic peak of g-C3N4 at 1120 cm−1 was assigned to the C-C stretching vibrations of the conjugated polymers [40]. The signature peaks of g-C3N4 at 711 cm−1 are attributed to the heptazine ring breathing mode of CN. After doping of the N atom, the peak at 711 cm−1 showed a redshift, which means the distortion of a heptazine ring structure. As shown in Fig. 2c, the introduction of GQDs caused an increased absorption within the range from 400 to 1000 nm and the absorption edge was red-shifted compared with that of C3N4. The bandgap energy (E g ) values were calculated from the Kubelka-Munk expression [41]:
(αhv)1/n = A(hv – Eg)
where α is the absorption index, h is the Planck constant, v is the frequency, Eg is the bandgap of semiconductor, and A is a constant. The E g of g-C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x was determined to be 2.7, 2.6, and 2.5 eV, respectively. AFM images (Figs. 2d and 2e) show the height of C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x films were 3.4 and 2.5 nm, respectively. The reduced thickness could shorten the distance for the excited electrons to reach the surface and prevent the recombination of electron and hole pairs. Since the theoretical thickness of monolayer g-C3N4 is 0.326 nm, AFM results imply that few-layer g-C3N4 film was successfully fabricated in our work.

XPS technique is used to investigate the surface electronic states and related interaction with the catalyst and the full XPS spectra of the carbon nitride series are shown in Fig. S3. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectra of C3N4 (Fig. 3a) had two peaks at 395.1 and 396.8 eV, which were attributed to the CN―C and N―H bonds, respectively. C3N4+x exhibited an additional XPS peak at 396.3 eV, suggesting an additional N―[C]3 bond. High-resolution C1s peaks (Fig. 3b) at 282.8 and 281.2 eV were attributed to N―CC and CC bonds, respectively [35]. After the incorporation of GQDs, the CC bond slightly shifted to higher binding energy compared with that of C3N4+x , due to the doping of carbon atoms into the cave of tri-triazine units.

The location of the conduction band and valence band, as well as the transportation process of the excited carriers, are illustrated in Scheme 1. The VB of g-C3N4 and C3N4+x is determined to be 1.8 eV while that of GQDs@C3N4+x is 1.9 eV (Fig. 3d). The CB could be estimated by ECB = EVB - Eg. Eg have been determined from the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Hence, the CB of g-C3N4, C3N4+x and g-C3N4 is estimated to be −0.9 eV, −0.8 eV, and −0.6 eV, respectively. Taking g-C3N4 into consideration, the HOMO is composed of 2p orbitals of low coordinated N atoms in planar while the LUMO is mainly composed of C 2p character in the Z direction. The obtained Eg value of C3N4 (2.7 eV) is much larger than the theoretical value of 2.1 eV [42], attributing to the incomplete polymerization and the existence of intermediate, e.g. melamine and melon carbon-containing compounds, etc. C3N4+x shows a slightly reduced Eg value of 2.6 eV possibly due to the N atom doping at the HOMO of Npz. Doping could decentralize the distribution of HOMO and LUMO, the decorated carbon atoms further reduced the Eg value to 2.5 eV. The smaller bandgap width means that solar energy can be utilized to a greater extent, thus, the catalytic performance could be improved effectively.

PL emission spectroscopy is used to investigate the behavior of excited electron-hole pairs. When excited wavelength at 385 nm, g-C3N4 showed a broad PL spectrum from 400 to 600 nm with two PL peaks at 434 and 470 nm, respectively (Fig. 3e). The luminescence process of g-C3N4 corresponds to the π*→π and π*→n radiative transition of the excited electrons. Compared with C3N4, C3N4+x exhibited an increased PL intensity, indicating that defects could influence the absorption efficiency and luminescence properties in 2D materials. The further increased fluorescence intensity after GQDs-decoration was attributed to the photoluminescence of GQDs [36,37]. Fluorescence lifetime is an intrinsic characteristic of luminescent material and could provide insight into the excited state dynamics. Hence, to gain a quantitative knowledge of the photogenerated charge carrier lifetime in all the carbon nitrogen serial samples, time-resolved fluorescence (TRF) spectra were measured (Fig. 3f). PL lifetime and PL intensity results that calculated by formula:
R (τ) = A1 exp (-τ/τ1) + A2 exp (-τ/τ2)

The average PL lifetime (τA) is calculated by the formula:
τA = (A1τ1 2 + A2τ2 2) / (A1τ1 + A2τ2)
τ
A
= A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
/
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2
,
where τ τ1 and τ τ2 are the short lifetime component and the long lifetime component, respectively, and A1 and A2 are the corresponding amplitudes [38]. The two components τ τ1 and τ τ2 stem from the non-radiative recombination of carriers in the surface defect states and inter-band recombination of the free excitons in the materials, respectively. The fitting results of PL spectrum are shown in Fig. S4 and the fitting lifetimes are summarized in Table 1. The short lifetime component τ1 for GQDs@C3N4+x (7.59 ns), which caused by the non-radiative recombination of the charge carriers was longer than those of C3N4 (6.66 ns). From another perspective, the PL lifetime τ τ is influenced by the radiative decay rate and the nonradiative decay rate. The presence of GQDs could improve the absorption efficiency and emission quantum efficiency in a broad range. Furthermore, the local field near GQDs could be enhanced leading to a larger radiative decay rate of the fluorophore. Consequently, as expected, the average lifetime (τA) of GQDs@C3N4+x was prolonged to 5.34 ns, showing that GQDs is conducive to suppressing the electron-hole recombination to improve the catalytic activity. Furthermore, the electron-transfer rate constant (ket) could be estimated according to the formula:
ket = 1/<τA>(C3N4) - 1/<τA>(GQDs@C3N4+x)
It is found that the ket of C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x was 2.2 × 107 S−1 and 2.8 × 107 S−1, respectively. It further provides evidence to explain the mechanism of lifetime extension.

Electrochemical measurements of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x in 0.1 M NaOH solution for water splitting were investigated in Fig. 4 and Fig. S5. The linear scan voltammetry (LSV, Fig. 4a) characterization was carried out at the potential range from 0.4 to 2.5 V vs. RHE with the scan rate of 100 mV s−1. Compared to C3N4 electrode, C3N4+x electrode showed an improved current density in electrocatalytic reaction. In water oxidation, the pyridinic-N of g-C3N4 serves as an active site for reaction [43]. The additional N atoms introduce defects into the triazine ring of C3N4, providing much more active sites for electrochemical reactions. The onset potential is an important parameter that affects the water oxidation performance of photoanodes. Usually, most articles use the potential at the intersection point of the dark current and the tangent at the maximum slope of the photocurrent to calculate the onset potential [44]. After the incorporation of CQDs, the onset potential revealed an obvious negative shift from 1.70 V to 1.35 V compared with that of C3N4+x . What’s more, the GQDs@C3N4+x electrode exhibited reduced value of Tafel plot, which indicating an increase in catalytic efficiency. GQDs show many peculiar characteristics in electrolysis owing to their remarkable quantum confinement, highly exposed surface, and edge effects [45,46]. Hence, GQDs could act as electron reservoirs and form delocalized π bonds. The π-π conjugate is beneficial for carrier transfer and can increase the electrical conductivity of g-C3N4+x .

As a non-destructive method, AC impedance spectroscopy is a promising technique for the characterization of nanomaterials. In general, a typical impedance spectrum for nanomaterials consists of high and low-frequency arcs, characterizing the bulk materials effect and polarization effect of electrode/specimen, respectively. The fitting results were shown in Fig. S6 and the equivalent circuit impedance was inserted in Fig. 4b. The fitting results of the equivalent circuit impedance analysis were shown in Table 2. The Rs represents the resistance value of the external circuit (including the liquid connection resistance and the total resistance generated by the external circuit). W represents Warburg impedance, which is caused by diffusion and usually appears at a low frequency. The radius of the semicircle is R1 and R1 represents the charge transfer impedance (Rct) generated during the catalytic reaction process. Rct is an important parameter for analyzing the catalytic activity of different catalytic materials. The reduced Rct of GQDs@C3N4+x was 42.14 Ω compared with those of C3N4 (117.7 Ω) and C3N4+x (128.8 Ω), illustrating the better catalytic performance. The heterojunction that forms at the interface between the GQDs and carbon nitride results in the effective separation of generated carriers leading to the enhancement of electrocatalytic efficiency [47]. Fig. 4c shows the Mott-Schottky plots for the C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x electrodes. The plots of electrodes all have a positive slope that indicates the carbon nitride is an n-type semiconductor. Generally speaking, the donor concentration (Nd) of the materials was obtained from the linear section of the Mott-Schottky plots. The charge carrier concentrations were calculated according to the Mott-Schottky plot. The Mott-Schottky equations were following:
1/C2 = (2/Nde0ε0ε) [ (E - EFB) - kT/e0]
Nd = (2/e0ε0ε) {[d(1/C2)/dV]−1}
where C, Nd, E and EFB were the charge capacitance, carrier density, applied potential, and flat band potential, respectively; e0, ε and ε0 refer to the charge constant (1.60 × 10−19 C), dielectric constant (7–8 for g-C3N4) and permittivity of a vacuum (8.85 × 10−12 F m−1); k is the Boltzmann constant (1.38 × 10−23 J K−1); and T is the temperature [48]. After calculation, the Nd value of the GQDs@C3N4+x (2.96 × 1018 cm−3) was the highest among those of the C3N4 (8.41 × 1017 cm−3) and C3N4+x (9.11 × 1017 cm−3), indicating the effective separation of the charge carriers to produce more active electrons for electrocatalysis. In conclusion, the presence of GQDs provides effective transport channels for carriers and effectively improves the conductivity of carbon nitride materials. The value of the flat band potential was defined by 1/C2 = 0. The EFB of g-C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x was determined to be − 1.1, − 1.0, and – 0.7 V vs. Ag/AgCl. For n-type semiconductors, the EFB was 0.2 eV higher than the conduction band (CB). Hence, the potentials of CB for g-C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x were estimated to be about −0.9, −0.8 and −0.5, which were consistent with the XPS calculation results. The stability tests of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs@C3N4+x were shown in Fig. 4d, illustrating that both the defect engineering and GQDs photosensitizer could effectively increase the electrochemical stability of intrinsic C3N4 in alkaline solution.

The electrolyte is one of the key components of an electrolysis cell, providing ionic conductivity and thus facilitating charge compensation on each electrode in the cell. Currently, the traditional oxygen generation reactions use alkaline electrolytes (eg. NaOH, KOH). Alkaline electrolytes could provide an abundance of OH- and the hot holes could scavenge by OH- ion, which is beneficial for the oxidation reaction for water splitting. OER mechanism in a basic electrolyte is as follows: 4OH- → O2+ 2 H2O + 4e- [49]. To investigate whether the electrolyte affects the overall electrochemical performance, the electrochemical tests of GQDs@C3N4+x electrode were carried out in different electrolytes (Fig. S7). In general, carbon nitride series electrodes could be used in different types of electrolytes, including neutral (NaF), acidic (H2SO4), alkaline (NaOH), redox-mediated (K4Fe(CN)6, Na2S2O3, Na2S/Na2S2O3), and organic (DHF, DMSO) solvents. Hence, various reagents have been used as additives in electrolytes. The current densities of carbon nitride electrodes at 1.8 V vs. RHE potential in different electrolytes were shown in Fig. 5a. Since the alkaline solution has been discussed, other electrolytes will be emphasized in this section. H2SO4 is the most common acid electrolyte for electrolysis mainly due to its very high ionic conductivity (0.8 S cm−1 for 1 M H2SO4 at 25 °C) [50]. The OER proceeds in the following step: 2 H2O → 4 H+ + 4e- + O2. The current density at 1.8 V vs. RHE in the H2SO4 electrolyte was higher than those in alkaline NaOH and neutral NaF electrolytes. The increased current density in the H2SO4 electrolyte is attributed to the fast-redox reactions that occurred on the particular surface functionalities, such as oxygenated carbon species. The neutral electrolyte has lower OH− concentrations compared to alkaline electrolytes, therefore a higher onset potential for oxygen evolution reactions can be expected. Meanwhile, organic electrolyte normally shows low ionic conductivity compared with acidic and alkaline electrolytes, leading to poor electrochemical performance. Therefore, extensive efforts have been devoted to exploring new redox-mediated materials.

To understand the effect of different ions on the performance of electrolysis, some comparative studies for redox-mediated electrolytes have also been investigated, including Na2S2O3, Na2S/Na2S2O3, and K4Fe(CN)6. K4[Fe(CN)6] is a versatile electron-transfer agent and can be involved in redox reactions through direct-transfer processes, such as from Fe(CN)6 4− to Fe(CN)6 3− [54,55]. Referring to K4Fe(CN)6 solution, the oxidation peak at 0.75 V vs. RHE was from the excited carriers participating in the redox reaction with Fe(CN)6 4- in the electrolyte (Fig. S8a). The current density on the GQDs@C3N4+x electrode in the K4Fe(CN)6 solution reached 0.4 mA cm−2 at 1.8 V vs. RHE. Besides, the electrodes in K4Fe(CN)6 could exhibit high capacitance due to the introduction of pseudocapacitance by the redox reactions of Fe(CN)6 4-/Fe(CN)6 3-. Notably, Fe(CN)6 4- or F- ions could be adsorbed onto the surface of the electrode and cause the cathodic shift of the onset potential. The adsorbed ions could occupy the surface-active sites of the catalyst, hindering the long-term stability of the electrode. Sulfur-redox reactions have been studied in various energy storage systems. Na2S2O3 is generally regarded as a hole scavenger and the electrochemical reactions that involve sulfide are called sulfite oxidation instead of water oxidation [51]. Na2S2O3 could react with the holes and oxidize to Na2SO4 and Na2S2O6 et al. [52]. Such as: Na2S2O3 + H2O → SO4 2- + 2Na+ + H2; Na2S2O3 + 2 H2O → S2O6 2- + 2OH- + H2. In the Na2S/Na2S2O3 electrolyte, the additional Na2S could enhance the stability of the Na2S2O3 solution. Besides, the Na2S could react with the surface defects or holes and get oxidized to sulfur-based species, such as 2Na2S → Na2S2 + 2Na+ + 2e-; S2- + 2 H2O+ 2e- →S2 2- + OH- + H2; Na2S + xS → Na2S x+1 to form Na2S2O4, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, and NaS x
[53]. As a result, carbon nitride electrodes showed enhancement of OER performance in Na2S2O3 and Na2S/Na2S2O3 redox-mediated electrolytes. The onset potential was reduced to 1.18 V by adding Na2S/Na2S2O3 redox-mediated in the water oxidation process. The onset potentials of the GQDs@C3N4+x electrode in different electrolytes were summarized in Fig. 5b. Compared to other literatures in Table S1, the GQDs@C3N4+x sample exhibited the lowest onset potential in Na2S/Na2S2O3 solution. The cathodic shift of onset potential could accelerate the water oxidation kinetics on an electrode. The current density on the GQDs@C3N4+x electrode in Na2S/Na2S2O3 solution was 0.3 mA cm−2 at 1.8 V vs. RHE (Fig. S8b). It should be noted that the photocurrent density of GQDs@C3N4+x sample was much lower than the maximum value reported in the literatures. Summarily, the carbon nitride materials exhibit the highest electrochemical performance in redox-mediated electrolytes compared with alkaline, acidic, and neutral electrolytes. The current in redox-mediated electrolyte consists of contributions not only from the electrode materials but also from the electrolyte. Hence, the reduction/oxidation of redox mediators in the electrolyte enhances the overall electrocatalytic performance.

To achieve rational improvement of electrolytes, additional electrochemical characterizations of carbon nitride series in redox-mediated (Na2S/Na2S2O3) electrolytes were carried out. The AC impedance of C3N4, C3N4+x and GQDs @C3N4+x in NaOH solution all showed semicircles at high-frequency region and lines at low-frequency region (Fig. 4b), due to the kinetics control of electrode and diffusion control of reactant of product, respectively. The AC impedance curves of the carbon nitride series show reduced charge transfer resistance (Rct) in Na2S/Na2S2O3 solution (Fig. 5c). The smaller Rct of GQDs@C3N4+x indicates a faster charge transfer rate. Besides, the Mott-Schottky plots of carbon nitride series in Na2S/Na2S2O3 solution exhibited increased carrier concentration (Fig. 5d) compared with those in NaOH solution. The lower onset potential and higher carrier density in redox-mediated electrolytes compared to alkaline electrolytes illustrate the higher electrocatalytic activity of the electrode materials. Hence, the electrolyte regulation strategy provides a viable approach for efficient energy conversion on metal-free electrocatalysts.

In summary, GQDs-decorated C3N4+x thin layers were synthesized by microwave-assisted approach. Thermal treatment with an additional N source introduced impurity N atoms into the g-C3N4 lattice and changed the blocky g-C3N4 to a porous and curled structure. The defect engineering is beneficial for the improvement of electrocatalytic activity. By engineering the GQDs at the interface of C3N4+x , better electrocatalytic performance was obtained. GQDs are regarded as electron reservoirs, which facilitate electron transfer and eventually improve the conductivity of 2D carbon nitride materials. This article also discusses how the electrolyte plays an important role in determining the outcome of the electrocatalytic process by investigating the electrocatalysis process which takes place at the electrocatalyst’s interface and the electrolyte. The carbon nitride serials electrodes exhibited effective enhancement of electrocatalytic performance in redox-mediated electrolytes compared with other electrolytes, including alkaline, acidic, neutral, and organic electrolytes. The redox reagent adsorbed on the surface of the electrode could lower the onset potential of the electrode and accelerate the water oxidation kinetics, thus improving the electrochemical performance of carbon nitride electrodes. The present work endeavors to provide insights into designing electrochemical devices and highlight the importance of rational defect engineering and electrolyte regulation.

Churong Ma: Resources, Formal analysis. Kai Chen: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Bo Yan: Validation, Formal analysis. Haiyue Zhuo: Validation. Ying Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Ying Liu reports financial support was provided by The national natural science foundation of China. Ying Liu reports financial support was provided by Basic and applied basic research project of Guangzhou. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62104081) and Basic and Applied Basic Research Project of Guangzhou (No. 202201010142).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.174323.
